original_hypothesis.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Cause Balance Inflation Leading to Last-Claimer Insolvency
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In `_processLock`, `balances[_receiver][_token] += _amount` credits the full requested amount, but for fee-on-transfer tokens, `safeTransferFrom` delivers fewer tokens than `_amount`. The contract's actual token balance becomes less than the sum of user balances. When the last users try to claim or withdraw, the contract lacks sufficient tokens, causing reverts.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The contract comment explicitly states 'ONLY add wrapped LRT tokens. Contract not compatible with rebase tokens' but does NOT mention fee-on-transfer incompatibility. The `allowToken` function can whitelist any ERC20 at the owner's discretion. If a fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted, all users of that token face insolvency risk.
deployment_assumptions: Owner must whitelist a fee-on-transfer token via allowToken()
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment script whitelists tokens (allowedTokens array is empty in script). Token whitelisting happens post-deployment via allowToken(). No evidence preventing fee-on-transfer token addition.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(_token).safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), _amount); then balances[_receiver][_token] += _amount — no balance delta check

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted via allowToken()
Multiple users try to withdraw or claim the same token

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Balance credited without checking actual received amount
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: balances[_receiver][_token] += _amount uses requested amount, not actual received
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No pre/post transfer balance check; no documentation of fee-on-transfer exclusion in code

analysis_questions: Are any of the target LRT tokens (weETH, ezETH, rsETH, rswETH, uniETH, pufETH) fee-on-transfer?
Could any of these tokens add fee-on-transfer behavior via upgrade?
What happens when last user's withdrawal or claim reverts?
validation_requirements: Deploy with a mock fee-on-transfer token
Verify last user's withdrawal reverts due to insufficient balance

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner whitelists a token with 1% transfer fee. Users deposit 100 tokens each. Contract receives 99 per deposit but credits 100. When later users withdraw, safeTransfer reverts for last users whose withdrawal exceeds the remaining contract balance.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Last depositors lose their tokens entirely; loss proportional to fee percentage * total deposits
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Late claimers/withdrawers of the affected token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partial — early users can withdraw, late users cannot

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing sum of balances vs contract token balance

validation_result.impact_summary: If a fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted via allowToken(), user balances will be inflated beyond the contract's actual holdings, causing last withdrawers/claimers to face reverts due to insufficient balance.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _processLock function at line ~193 performs `IERC20(_token).safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), _amount)` and then at line ~200 credits `balances[_receiver][_token] += _amount`. For a fee-on-transfer token, the contract receives less than `_amount` but credits the full `_amount`. Over multiple deposits, the divergence between actual token balance and sum of user balances grows. When later users call `withdraw()` (which does `IERC20(_token).safeTransfer(msg.sender, lockedAmount)`) or `_claim()` (which calls `_fillQuote` with the inflated amount), the contract will lack sufficient tokens, causing safeTransfer reverts. The code comment at `allowToken()` warns only about rebase tokens ('ONLY add wrapped LRT tokens. Contract not compatible with rebase tokens') but does NOT mention fee-on-transfer incompatibility. The `allowToken()` function has zero validation and accepts any address. This is a genuine code defect: the missing pre/post balance delta check is a well-known Solidity anti-pattern for ERC20 handling.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 193-200 of PrelaunchPoints.sol clearly show the pattern: safeTransferFrom for `_amount`, then crediting `_amount` without checking actual received. The withdraw function at lines 253-254 transfers the full `lockedAmount` back. The _claim function at line 268 uses `userClaim = userStake * _percentage / 100` based on the inflated balance. All three paths confirm the defect.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function credits `_amount` to the user's balance without verifying how many tokens were actually received. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives (_amount - fee) but records _amount, creating a solvency gap.
Attempts to transfer the full inflated `lockedAmount` back to the user. For the last withdrawers, the contract will not hold enough tokens, causing safeTransfer to revert.
Uses inflated userStake to calculate userClaim. The _fillQuote approves and attempts to swap more tokens than the contract actually holds for that user's share.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls lock(_token, _amount, _referral) where _token is a fee-on-transfer token whitelisted by owner
6. Multiple users deposit, each time gap between actual balance and recorded balances widens
9. IERC20(_token).safeTransfer(msg.sender, lockedAmount) reverts if contract lacks sufficient tokens (line 254)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability manifests across the deposit-to-withdrawal lifecycle. Each deposit via _processLock (line 193-200) inflates the accounting by the fee amount. The cumulative inflation means sum(balances[*][token]) > IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this)). When the last users attempt withdraw (line 254) or _claim (line 271), the safeTransfer/approve calls fail due to insufficient actual token balance, violating INV-ECON-003.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Owner must whitelist a fee-on-transfer token via allowToken()
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment script (PrelaunchPoints.s.sol) deploys with an empty allowedTokens array: `address[] public allowedTokens;`. Token whitelisting happens post-deployment via allowToken(). The allowToken function (line 313) has no validation against fee-on-transfer tokens - it simply sets `isTokenAllowed[_token] = true`. The code comment on allowToken says 'ONLY add wrapped LRT tokens. Contract not compatible with rebase tokens' but does NOT mention fee-on-transfer incompatibility. No on-chain enforcement prevents fee-on-transfer token addition.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is straightforward: (1) Owner whitelists a token with e.g. 1% transfer fee via allowToken(). (2) User A deposits 100 tokens - contract receives 99, credits 100. (3) User B deposits 100 tokens - contract receives 99, credits 100. Contract holds 198 tokens but records 200. (4) User A withdraws successfully (gets 100 tokens, contract has 98 left). (5) User B tries to withdraw 100 tokens but contract only has 98 - safeTransfer reverts. User B loses access to their tokens. The severity scales with the number of depositors and fee percentage.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If a fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted, the last users to withdraw or claim that token will be unable to do so. The loss equals approximately (fee_percentage * total_deposits) distributed as unrecoverable shortfall among last claimers. The recoverERC20 function cannot help because it explicitly blocks allowed tokens (line 326: `if (tokenAddress == address(lpETH) || isTokenAllowed[tokenAddress]) revert NotValidToken()`). Emergency mode enables LRT token withdrawals but doesn't fix the solvency gap.
recommended_mitigations: Use pre/post balance delta pattern: `uint256 balBefore = IERC20(_token).balanceOf(address(this)); IERC20(_token).safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), _amount); uint256 actualReceived = IERC20(_token).balanceOf(address(this)) - balBefore; balances[_receiver][_token] += actualReceived;`
Add explicit documentation and/or on-chain checks in allowToken() to reject known fee-on-transfer tokens
Consider adding a token compatibility check function that verifies transfer amount equals received amount before allowing a token
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Create a MockFeeToken ERC20 with 1% transfer fee. 2. Deploy PrelaunchPoints with empty allowedTokens. 3. Owner calls allowToken(MockFeeToken). 4. User A approves and calls lock(MockFeeToken, 100e18, 0x0). Verify balances[A][token]=100e18 but contract holds 99e18. 5. User B does the same. Verify balances sum=200e18 but contract holds 198e18. 6. User A calls withdraw(MockFeeToken) - succeeds, gets 100e18 (contract debited 100, has 98 left). 7. User B calls withdraw(MockFeeToken) - reverts because contract only has 98e18 but tries to transfer 100e18.
uncertainties: The intended LRT tokens (weETH, ezETH, rsETH, rswETH, uniETH, pufETH) are not currently fee-on-transfer tokens, so this requires owner misconfiguration or future token upgrades
Some of these LRT tokens may be upgradeable proxies that could theoretically add fee-on-transfer behavior in the future
The practical likelihood of the owner whitelisting a fee-on-transfer token is unknown but is not prevented by code
additional_testing_needed: Verify whether any of the target LRT tokens are upgradeable and could add fee-on-transfer behavior
Test the exact revert behavior when the last user's withdrawal exceeds contract balance
Test interaction between partial claims (_percentage < 100) and fee-on-transfer accounting gaps
